key_change: Synthesis states Bretton Woods collapse shows gold rising after severance, but understates that the 1971-74 price surge

challenge_points.description: Synthesis states Bretton Woods collapse shows gold rising after severance, but understates that the 1971-74 price surge was also driven by simultaneous inflation and dollar devaluation, not solely regime shift.
Core claim of safe-haven thesis breakdown rests on one investing.com commentary without data tables, controls, or post-2022 updates on recent conflicts.
Dollar-gold inverse correlation during crises is noted but not examined through futures positioning or COT data that could explain mechanical selloffs.
Link between the Edge-Forex safe-haven correlation and the Nixon shock sources is not drawn to test whether post-1971 floating regime strengthened or weakened that correlation.

first_pass_summary: The research sessions heavily concentrated on a single historical episode—the collapse of the Bretton Woods system (1971)—while largely failing to address the original research question about why gold drops during geopolitical stress. Across six sessions, the Nixon shock and Bretton Woods collapse appeared repeatedly (referenced in nearly every session), but this event represents gold rising after the dollar-gold link was severed, not the counterintuitive drops the researcher sought to understan…
